articles.title: England: Government “remains of the view” that the International Student Levy should go ahead; implementation planned for August 2028
articles.summary: The UK government confirmed in September 2025 that it will implement an International Student Levy of £925 per international student, to be charged as a flat fee to higher education providers. The levy is scheduled to take effect in August 2028, following its initial proposal in the May 2025 immigration white paper.
articles.insight:
The UK’s planned £925 International Student Levy—set for August 2028—may modestly dampen institutional willingness to recruit international students, particularly from lower-margin markets, though its direct impact on student demand or visa policy remains uncertain pending final regulations and sector response.  
Admissions teams should track how UK universities adjust recruitment targets, scholarship offerings, and regional outreach in response to the levy, while benchmarking against competitor nations’ fee structures and immigration incentives over the next 12–18 months.

articles.title: “The tsunami keeps coming”: US leaders react to final D/S rule
articles.summary: The U.S. Department of Homeland Security announced the termination of duration of status (D/S) for certain nonimmigrant visa categories, a policy change associated with the Trump administration. U.S. leaders criticized the move as an “arbitrary intrusion” into higher education, and the reaction was reported by The PIE News under the headline “The tsunami keeps coming”: US leaders react to final D/S rule.
articles.insight:
This policy shift may dampen U.S. appeal for students from countries where D/S applied—especially in STEM and exchange programs—though its direct impact on overall demand remains uncertain amid broader geopolitical and economic factors influencing student mobility.  
Admissions teams should track enrolment trends among affected visa holders (e.g., J-1, F-1 in specific fields), consult legal advisors on compliance implications, and assess whether messaging around visa stability needs updating in outreach to key source markets.

articles.title: UK VAT on private schools triggers record decline in Hong Kong students
articles.summary: UK private schools saw a record decline in Hong Kong student enrolment after the UK imposed a 20 per cent VAT on school fees, resulting in a 17 per cent overall drop in enrolment and a 26 per cent fall in new admissions. According to an annual census by the UK’s Independent Schools Council, enrolment has reverted to pre-emigration wave levels as Hong Kong parents become more selective amid rising costs.
articles.insight:
This news signals potential sensitivity among Hong Kong families to sudden cost increases in education destinations, suggesting demand may shift toward jurisdictions with stable fee structures and clear value propositions—though broader geopolitical and economic factors remain stronger drivers than VAT alone.  
Admissions teams should monitor Hong Kong enrolment trends across their institutions, assess fee transparency and scholarship offerings, and track whether similar policy changes emerge in other key source markets or destination countries.

articles.title: DSE candidates overcome physical challenges to achieve hard-earned results
articles.summary: Tam Chun-yu, a 20-year-old non-verbal student at the Hong Kong Red Cross John F. Kennedy Centre, achieved DSE results of level 2 in English, Chinese, and information and communication technology (ICT), and level 3 in Chinese, typing all answers with one finger. His total score of seven points out of 42 was announced on Wednesday as part of the official DSE results release.
articles.insight:
This story highlights growing visibility of students with diverse abilities from China achieving academic milestones despite significant physical barriers, potentially signalling heightened interest among families in inclusive, supportive overseas education environments—though direct demand shifts remain unconfirmed without broader data.  
Admissions teams should monitor Chinese social media and education forums for emerging narratives around disability-inclusive admissions, while reviewing accessibility provisions and outreach messaging to ensure alignment with evolving expectations of equity and support.

articles.title: Decline in Indian demand a major factor in softer outlook for foreign enrolment in US higher education in 2026/27
articles.summary: The Institute of International Education (IIE) conducts a twice-yearly snapshot survey of US institutions to track international enrolment trends, and this year’s 2026 Spring Snapshot is considered especially critical due to ongoing market and policy shifts. A decline in demand from Indian students is identified as a major factor contributing to the softer outlook for foreign enrolment in US higher education for the 2026/27 academic year.
articles.insight:
A notable dip in Indian student demand for US institutions—potentially reflecting shifting preferences, affordability concerns, or policy impacts—may signal broader recalibrations in global student mobility, though it remains unclear whether this represents a temporary correction or longer-term realignment across English-speaking destinations.  
Admissions teams should track Indian application trends across their own pipelines, monitor H-1B and post-study work policy developments in key markets, and assess how peer institutions are adjusting outreach—while avoiding premature assumptions about spillover effects to other source regions.

articles.title: Vietnam mandates AI and digital skills for all university graduates
articles.summary: Vietnam’s Ministry of Education and Training has issued a new regulation requiring all university students to graduate with digital competencies and knowledge of artificial intelligence. This is the first time these skills have been mandated as compulsory learning outcomes for undergraduate programs in the country.
articles.insight:
Vietnam’s new mandate signals growing domestic prioritisation of AI and digital literacy, potentially reshaping student expectations around curriculum relevance and global employability—though direct impacts on outbound mobility remain uncertain without evidence of shifting preferences or policy incentives.  
Admissions teams should monitor Vietnamese student inquiries for increased emphasis on AI-related coursework, tech-integrated pedagogy, or career outcomes, and assess whether their programmes explicitly articulate digital skill development to align with evolving national graduate standards.

articles.title: Southeast Asia's No.2 economy extends work permits for 770,000 foreigners to ease labor shortages
articles.summary: The Thai Cabinet approved an extension of work permits for approximately 770,000 migrant workers from Laos, Myanmar, and Vietnam. This measure aims to address labor shortages in Thailand’s manufacturing and services sectors and support ongoing economic activity.
articles.insight:
This news signals potential softening in Thailand’s appeal as a regional study destination for students from Laos, Myanmar, and Vietnam, as extended work permits may strengthen local employment incentives over overseas education—though direct enrolment impact remains uncertain without data on student mobility trends.  
Admissions teams should monitor Thai university enrolment patterns among these three nationalities and track whether extended work rights correlate with declining visa applications or shifts toward vocational pathways, adjusting outreach messaging accordingly.

articles.title: Breaking: US Department of Homeland Security publishes rule to end Duration of Status for international students
articles.summary: The US Department of Homeland Security has published a final rule ending Duration of Status (D/S) for F and J nonimmigrant students and their dependents. The final rule includes only minor revisions from the August 2025 proposal, despite extensive lobbying from scientific, business, and higher education sectors.
articles.insight:
This rule signals potential tightening of US student visa flexibility, which may dampen demand from risk-averse or compliance-sensitive applicants—though actual enrolment impact depends on implementation timing, alternative destinations’ policies, and how institutions mitigate perceived instability.  
Admissions teams should track early application trends from key markets like India and China, review messaging around US visa certainty, and assess whether competitors in the UK, Australia, or NZ are positioning themselves as more predictable alternatives amid evolving immigration narratives.

articles.title: Beyond Dubai’s Bling, Chinese Students See a Future
articles.url: https://www.sixthtone.com/news/1018717/Beyond Dubai’s Bling, Chinese Students See a Future
articles.summary: Chinese students in the UAE are increasingly choosing to study there primarily for access to affordable international education rather than being attracted by Dubai’s luxury or glamour. This shift is prompting them to reconsider their perspectives on education, career paths, and future opportunities.
articles.insight:
This trend signals growing demand among Chinese students for cost-effective, globally recognized education outside traditional Anglophone destinations, potentially reflecting shifting perceptions of value and risk in international study—though it remains unclear whether this represents a sustained pivot or short-term adaptation to visa or economic pressures.  
Admissions teams should monitor enrollment patterns from China into UAE institutions, especially in STEM and business programs, while assessing whether affordability, regional stability, and post-study work pathways are becoming decisive factors in student decision-making alongside brand prestige.
